title: Fixed-detector tilt--defocus sensing by upstream source coding in a time-reversed Young interferometer
abstract: We propose a physically explicit sensing application of a time-reversed Young (TRY) interferometer: simultaneous monitoring of beam tilt and focus drift with a fixed detector. The task is relevant to compact optical relays, free-space links, fiber-coupling stages, and micro-optical alignment modules, where continuous tracking of pointing and focus is needed but downstream wavefront cameras or multiport analyzers are undesirable. Using a finite-width double-slit Fresnel model, we derive the exact local TRY response functions for tilt-like and defocus-like phase perturbations and compute the corresponding optimal upstream source codes numerically. The physical optimal codes are fringe-locked and differ qualitatively from the simple odd/even modes suggested by Gaussian toy models. Two source-coded scalar channels recover essentially all local Fisher information in the full source-resolved TRY record for the physical model considered here. Compared with downstream direct intensity sensing, TRY provides first-order access to the mixed tilt--defocus task with fixed detection; compared with ideal downstream matched-mode sorting, its advantage is architectural rather than fundamental.
summary: The paper proposes a method to monitor beam tilt and defocus using a fixed detector by applying optimized 'source codes' upstream in a Young interferometer. It proves that using physically accurate Fresnel-based codes enables near-perfect information recovery, whereas traditional Gaussian approximations fail significantly.
main_problem: Developing an efficient method for simultaneous monitoring of beam tilt and focus drift in compact optical systems using a fixed-detector architecture.
main_result: Optimized upstream source codes derived from a Fresnel model allow for near-lossless information compression (retaining >99.98% of Fisher information) into just two scalar channels, significantly outperforming simple Gaussian-based toy models.
method: The study uses a Fisher Information Matrix formalism to quantify information retention and employs a noise-weighted Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization process to derive optimal, fringe-locked source codes.
model_or_system: A Time-Reversed Young (TRY) interferometer based on a monochromatic scalar field and a finite-width double-slit Fresnel diffraction model.
key_observables: Tilt-like and defocus-like phase perturbations, local response functions (sensitivity maps), and coded scalar observables.
important_parameters: Wavelength (633 nm), slit width and separation, propagation distances, and the shot-noise-limited detection regime.
main_assumptions: Small phase perturbations (linearized regime), monochromatic scalar field, and shot-noise-limited detection with a regularizing background floor.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the baseline TRY intensity response, the exact local score functions for tilt and defocus, and the numerically optimized, oscillatory, fringe-locked source codes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the sensing problem, defines the physical Fresnel model, derives the analytical response functions, implements the upstream source-coding optimization, and concludes with a comparative information-theoretic analysis against downstream sensing and Gaussian models.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and precision measurement, as it demonstrates how architectural changes (moving analysis upstream) can enable high-precision tracking of wavefront aberrations without complex downstream hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Physical TRY response and optimal source codes for the finite-width Fresnel model. (a) Baseline source-resolved TRY response R0(y). (b) Exact local score functions gt(y) and gf(y) for tilt and defocus. (c) Numerically optimized nuisance-orthogonal source codes. The physical optimal codes are oscillatory and locked to the real TRY fringe structure rather than to a smooth odd/even toy basis.
